title: Orchestrate credential profiles and ergonomic pullstream bridging
preview:
Context: We've had ongoing divergence across the Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs for credential fallbacks, retry presets, and the pullstream (streaming) ergonomics. Teams reported brittle reauth interactions during long-lived streams and inconsistent retry defaults across SDKs leading to different production behavior. This PR centralizes the credential profile definitions, introduces an ergonomic PullStreamBridge to harmonize consumer-facing streaming patterns, and bumps tri-SDK versions to align expectations.

Motivation: 1) Make credential fallback behavior predictable across languages (delegated, cascade, and local-cache modes). 2) Provide clear retry presets (latency-sensitive, throughput-optimized, and conservative) that map to runtime policies. 3) Offer a small, opt-in PullStreamBridge API that simplifies checkpointing/resume, peek semantics, and a cancelable iterator pattern that matches the new credential lease/heartbeat semantics.

What this PR does (summary): - New shared credential profile catalog (internal/cred/profile_catalog.go + language bindings) describing TTLs, refresh windows, and fallback order. - DelegatedFallback credential mode: prefer delegated tokens, then fall back to cached service token, then to refresh. - Retry presets: latency-sensitive (single fast reauth attempt, short backoff), throughput-optimized (aggressive batching-friendly backoff), conservative (safer but higher tail latency). Presets are serialized in a small JSON schema used to drive SDK defaults. - PullStreamBridge: small facade implemented in each SDK that provides peek(), next(), and resume_from(checkpoint) semantics; attaches credential lease heartbeats when streams stay idle for >30s. - Telemetry tags added to all streaming requests: sdk.language, cred.profile, retry.preset, stream.bridge=true. - Tests and integration harness updates for session handoff scenarios and long-lived streams. - Version bumps: python 0.14.4, typescript 1.6.3, go 1.5.1 (changelogs included).

Files changed (high-level): see files_changed list below; notable changes include SDK public surface additions (peekable/resume methods), internal credential orchestrator, JSON presets, and docs/upgrade-guides/sdk-credential-orchestrator.md.

Important design notes: - The credential profile catalog is intentionally small and serializable so the control plane can propose presets in the future; SDKs read the local defaults but accept a remote override object. - PullStreamBridge is opt-in: existing streaming semantics are preserved unless consumers import the bridge facade. This keeps the PR safe for large dependent codebases. - Retry presets intentionally map to route/runtime hints; the runtime already understands the preset names and can route requests to different capacity tiers when needed.

Testing: - Unit tests added for all SDKs (python tests/unit/test_retry_profiles.py, ts tests/stream/pullstream_bridge.spec.ts, go stream/facade_test.go). - Integration scenario in perf-canary updated to run a long-lived stream with delegated credential rotation. - CI matrix ran py/ts/go, linting, and e2e harness; one flaky TS test was stabilized by adding a short jitter to timing expectations.

How to try locally: - python: pip install -e sdk/python && run examples/streaming/pullstream_example.py --use-bridge
- typescript: yarn install && yarn test:stream --bridge
- go: go test ./... -run PullStreamBridge; example in examples/go/pullstream/README.md

Rollout plan: feature gated behind environment variable REDWOOD_SDK_USE_PULLSTREAM_BRIDGE for initial weeks. We coordinated with infra (ENG-1951) to ensure runtime preset semantics are honored.

Related work: references ENG-1951 (credential orchestration), ENG-2022 (streaming ergonomics spike), and SUP-4123 (customer incident describing token rotation mid-stream).

Release notes: added to changelogs for each SDK; see release_notes field below.

Merge: squash merge after final approval and successful CI rerun.

title: Graceful disconnect: buffered tool-call retry and reconnect window for streaming API
preview: Motivation: We observed customer incidents where short-lived client disconnects during multi-step function/tool-calling flows caused duplicate tool invocations or orphaned streaming sessions, leading to higher costs and inconsistent structured outputs. This PR introduces a server-side in-flight buffer and a short reconnect window with idempotency reconciliation to preserve end-to-end semantics while keeping latency and cost in check. Summary of changes: 1) Session manager: add a buffered-inflight queue that retains in-progress tool invocations for a configurable reconnect window (default 20s). Sessions now emit a compact session token (reconnect_token) on streaming start. 2) Tool invocation idempotency: introduce X-Tool-Invocation-Id header and runtime-side idempotency table so duplicate replays are deduplicated; tools should return idempotency-safe responses. 3) Reconnect/reconcile flow: upon client reconnect with reconnect_token, server attempts to reconcile buffered inflight work with the client's last-ack position, returning any accumulated partial structured output and the current tool invocation states. If the client reconnects after the window, buffered items are aborted with graceful teardown events. 4) Soft-cancel semantics: new soft-cancel signal allows clients to request an in-flight tool call be aborted only if it has not reached an irreversible side-effect point; otherwise the server completes the call and returns final structured output. 5) SDK/compat: TypeScript and Python SDKs added helper APIs to expose reconnect_token and soft-cancel semantics while preserving OpenAI-compatible streaming events (partial chunks, function_call start/finish events). 6) Observability: new metrics (session.reconnect_attempts, tool.invocation.dedup_hits, session.buffered_items) and traces to aid debugging. 7) Tests: unit tests for reconcile/timeout logic, integration tests simulating flaky client disconnects, and an e2e test that ensures no duplicate tool executions for retries. Checklist: - [x] API design reviewed for OpenAI compatibility - [x] Runtime changes gated behind feature flag streaming_reconnect_v2 - [x] SDK helpers implemented and docstrings added - [x] Metrics and tracing added - [x] Tests included and green on CI Design notes: reconnect_token is short-lived (default TTL 20s) to avoid long-lived server buffering. The buffered queue is size-capped per-session and uses LRU eviction for low-memory impact; overflow causes immediate abort with structured error to client. Soft-cancel is advisory to the runtime and will translate to an AbortableContext inside tool runners; tools can opt-in to fast abort or honor idempotency markers and complete. Compatibility: OpenAI-compatible streaming events are preserved: the server emits a function_call.start event when a tool is invoked, intermittent partial function_call.content chunks during streaming, and function_call.finish when completed; on reconnect the server will stream any missed chunks and then resume new output. CI notes: initial run flagged a flaky e2e test that relied on timing; test was stabilized by switching to event-based waits and re-run. Merge: squash-merged to main on 2026-03-03 after approvals. Impact: reduces duplicate tool calls in flaky network scenarios, gives SDKs a clear reconnect primitive, and improves observability for streaming interruptions. Runtime defaults keep memory/cost bounded and the feature is controlled by feature flags for gradual rollout.

title: implement signal smoothing and traffic-granularity guard for noisy tail regressions
preview:
Motivation: Recent canary runs showed recurrent false-positive tail-latency alerts during short traffic spikes, and we observed noisy variance for small-batch inference workloads. This PR introduces a two-part defense: 1) a lightweight temporal signal smoother that reduces high-frequency noise in per-route latency fingerprints while preserving changepoints, and 2) a traffic-granularity guard that slices canary evaluations by traffic orthogonals (route, payload-size bucket, and client tier) and gates rollouts when small, concentrated cohorts drive apparent regressions.

Goals: reduce alert churn, avoid unnecessary rollbacks for bursty micro-cohorts, and improve detection power for sustained regressions. This is intended as a conservative enhancement — default behavior is opt-in per-alert and the rollout guard has configurable thresholds and cooldown periods.

What changed (high-level): - Add exponential moving quantile smoother (EQS) and heteroskedasticity-aware window sizing to the canary metric stream processor. EQS adapts window length based on recent variance estimates so long-lived trends are preserved but transient spikes are suppressed. - Add traffic-granularity slicing to the canary evaluator. Evaluations now compute cohort-level fingerprints for route/payload_size/client_tier and will only escalate if either (a) a majority-weighted cohort shows sustained degradation, or (b) a global signal exceeds a tunable severity threshold. - New staged-fallback policy: on cohort-only degradation, the orchestrator applies a soft cork (reduce canary traffic by N%) and schedules a strengthened probe set; on global degradation the system triggers standard rollback. - Exposed config knobs in canary config and Redwood Console: smoothing_alpha, variance_thresholds, cohort_min_samples, staged_cork_percentage, staged_cork_cooldown. - Unit + perf tests and a small runbook for oncall to interpret cohort-level alerts.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of EQS and cohort slicing in canary processor
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing logic and cohort aggregation
- [x] Integration test with synthetic burst traffic profile
- [x] Console schema changes + docs entry
- [x] Runbook and alert text updates

Backward compatibility and safety: defaults preserve existing behavior when smoothing_alpha=0 and cohort_min_samples is large. The cohort guard is disabled by default for customers not enrolled in perf-canary v2.

Related: Linear ENG-1847 (reduce false positive canary alerts), ENG-1912 (cohort-aware canary rollouts), SUP-4123 (customer support ticket tying to bursty small-batch inference).

title: Consensus-backed metadata rollup and reader-friendly changelog
preview:
Context and motivation: We onboard models from a variety of sources (author-uploaded manifests, CI-provided metadata, and discovery probes). Discrepancies between sources (missing fields, differing perf metrics, variant aliases) have caused noisy changelogs and occasional release regressions. This PR implements a deterministic "consensus rollup" step during intake that: 1) canonicalizes manifest keys and field formats; 2) merges performance profile vectors using a configurable aggregator (weighted median by signal confidence); 3) records provenance for each field so downstream systems can explain where a value came from; and 4) emits a concise, reader-friendly changelog "teaser" that groups changes into: metadata edits, perf deltas, and variant-level lifecycle events.

What changed (high level):
- New consensus rollup service (registry/rollup) that takes a set of manifests and returns a single canonical manifest plus a changelog fragment.
- Aggregator implementations: weighted_median (default) and fallback_mean (opt-in).
- Field provenance map: compact provenance stamps stored alongside canonical fields for traceability.
- Changelog formatter (registry/changelog/teaser) that outputs a short prose summary plus machine tags for UI tooling.
- Intake pipeline wiring: the onboarding task now calls rollup before persisting model versions.
- Tests: unit tests for canonicalization rules, aggregator edge cases, and an end-to-end integration test that runs a small sample intake and asserts changelog shape.

Commits (summary):
- 8a7b2c1: add consensus rollup service and initial aggregator APIs
- 7f92d4e: implement weighted_median aggregator and provenance stamps
- f03bd9a: changelog teaser formatter and grouping rules
- c4a1b2e: hook rollup into intake task; add integration test harness
- d2f4e51: docs: onboarding guide updates and examples
- 1b8fa6c: fix: handle variant alias collisions; add extra unit tests

Files touched (high level):
- registry/rollup/service.py (new)
- registry/rollup/aggregators.py (new)
- registry/changelog/teaser.py (new)
- registry/intake/handler.py (modified)
- tests/registry/test_rollup.py (new)
- tests/registry/integration_test_sample_intake.py (new)
- docs/registry/onboarding.md (modified)
- pkg/schemas/model_manifest.yaml (modified)
- scripts/sample_manifests/*.json (added examples)

Review conversation (high-level excerpt):
- Ben Carter: "Great direction. Please clarify the conflict-resolution priority list in the docs and add a test for missing confidence scores." (requested changes)
- Aisha Patel (author): "Added explicit priority table to docs and added a test that defaults missing confidence to 0.4. Also documented how to opt into fallback_mean." (addressed)
- Lina Morales: "Can we guarantee aggregator determinism across Python versions? Consider sorting keys before aggregation." (comment)
- Aisha Patel: "Added key sorting and a deterministic float reduction step; included a note and added a regression test: tests/registry/test_rollup.py::test_aggregator_determinism." (addressed)
- Tomas Ilyin: "LGTM after CI green; small nits inline about log level and a type hint." (approved)

CI summary: Initial run showed one flaky integration test (tests/registry/integration_test_sample_intake.py::test_end_to_end) failing on timeout in the mock persistence layer. I reran the job; subsequent run passed. All checks reported green: lint, unit tests, integration, and build. Coverage slightly decreased in an unrelated helper module (acceptable per ENG-1934).

Backward compatibility and risk: No user-facing API changes. The canonical manifest schema was minimally extended with an optional provenance map; schema validations accept the new optional field. Intake path is feature-flagged on rollout; default enabled for new onboardings. The rollup introduces deterministic merging rules to reduce churn in release notes and should decrease noise. If a downstream system depends on specific field values from a single source, we document how to opt-out and pin source priority.

Testing and validation: Unit tests cover aggregator edge cases (empty lists, single-source, conflicting confidences). Integration test runs sample intake with three synthetic manifests and asserts canonical output and a changelog fragment with expected grouped headings. Perf: aggregator runs in ~3–7ms for typical manifests (measured in CI microbenchmark script). Added alerts if rollup latency exceeds 200ms in production.

Docs and examples: docs/registry/onboarding.md updated with a short example showing input manifests and resulting teaser. Added scripts/sample_manifests to allow reproducing the integration test locally.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged to main on 2026-03-02 after approvals. Post-merge actions: 1) feature flag rollout started in staging; 2) we scheduled a small canary sweep to validate changelog quality on ~50 recent onboardings (ENG-2041).

Notes for release engineers: There is a small database migration to add the optional provenance column (backfilled as NULL). Migration script included: db/migrations/20260302_add_provenance_column.sql. Rollback is safe (column optional).

title: Passive prefill operator and econ blueprint for safe defaults
preview:
Motivation: Many customers want lower per-token unit economics without increasing tail latency. Existing aggressive prefill strategies improved cost but occasionally increased 95/99p latency during cold tails or introduced cache pollution. This PR introduces a passive prefill operator (runtime) plus an 'econ operator' blueprint (console + defaults) to provide safe, opt-in cost defaults that preserve latency SLOs.

What this PR does: implements a passive prefill path that populates KV/prefix caches in the background without blocking client responses, adds an econ operator that enforces budget windows and caps (max-prefill-tokens, adaptive-mini-batch limits), and ships a console blueprint with vetted defaults for common workloads (chat, single-turn generation, embeddings). The blueprint exposes: safe batch/timeout profiles, quantization profile recommendations, and a fallback policy that routes to a higher-cost low-latency tier when latency risk is detected.

Files changed: runtime prefill operator, econ operator enforcement module, console blueprint docs, CLI toggles, integration tests, and CI smoke configs.

Checklist: [x] passive prefill runtime implementation, [x] econ operator rules engine + metric hooks, [x] unit & integration tests, [x] perf benchmarks vs baseline, [x] blueprints & docs, [x] feature flag + canary rollout plan.

Performance/telemetry summary: In our internal benchmark (multi-model chat mix with 8k median context): enabling passive prefill + econ defaults reduced median token cost by ~6.5% and 99p cost by ~7.9% while adding +0.3% median latency and +0.9% 99p latency in worst-case cold start scenarios. Shadow-telemetry mode confirms no spike in error rates.

Rollout plan: feature gated (runtime flag: runtime.passive_prefill.enabled=false by default). Stage 1: shadow mode on main for 48h. Stage 2: canary on 5% of traffic in eu-west for 72h. Stage 3: gradual ramp with monitoring dashboards and automatic rollback on latency SLO breach.

Why this is different: rather than forcing prefill synchronously, the passive operator decouples cache population from client-critical path and pairs it with an econ operator that keeps cost reductions within conservative safety envelopes. This matches Redwood's production-first thesis: predictable economics, observable regressions, and safe defaults for teams that don't want to tune batching/quantization manually.

Checklist: [x] passive prefill runtime implementation, [x] econ operator rules engine + metric hooks, [x] unit & integration tests, [x] perf benchmarks vs baseline, [x] blueprints & docs, [x] feature flag + canary rollout plan.

Performance/telemetry summary: In our internal benchmark (multi-model chat mix with 8k median context): enabling passive prefill + econ defaults reduced median token cost by ~6.5% and 99p cost by ~7.9% while adding +0.3% median latency and +0.9% 99p latency in worst-case cold start scenarios. Shadow-telemetry mode confirms no spike in error rates.

Rollout plan: feature gated (runtime flag: runtime.passive_prefill.enabled=false by default). Stage 1: shadow mode on main for 48h. Stage 2: canary on 5% of traffic in eu-west for 72h. Stage 3: gradual ramp with monitoring dashboards and automatic rollback on latency SLO breach.

Why this is different: rather than forcing prefill synchronously, the passive operator decouples cache population from client-critical path and pairs it with an econ operator that keeps cost reductions within conservative safety envelopes. This matches Redwood's production-first thesis: predictable economics, observable regressions, and safe defaults for teams that don't want to tune batching/quantization manually.

title: prevent-downgrades-and-standardize-on-disk-backup-format
preview:
Motivation: Past upgrades have exposed two recurring problems for private deployments: accidental downgrades (operator error or rollback scripts that do not account for schema/feature flags) and inconsistent backup formats across installer versions which makes restores brittle. This PR introduces a lightweight downgrade-blocker in the installer, pins on-disk backup metadata to a stable v2 format, and adds a deterministic restore test to the CI matrix.

Summary of changes: Added a check in the private installer that refuses to apply a release with an older semantic version if that release would require a schema downgrade; the check is driven by a new release-metadata.json (version + schema_version + migration_path). Implemented a stable on-disk backup v2 format (single tar.gz with manifests/etcd-snapshots/secrets.enc and a compact manifest.json) and utilities to produce/consume it. Introduced an idempotent schema-migration runner with pre/post hooks and automatic dry-run mode. Hooked backup/restore smoke into CI to validate cross-version restores (main->v1.3 pin and back). Updated terraform and helm manifests to pin provider/module versions used by the installer so backups produced during restore tests are reproducible.

What this change does (concrete): 1) installer/upgrade/downgrade_blocker.go: adds logic that compares incoming release metadata to cluster state and rejects operations that would lower schema_version or remove required migrations. 2) installer/backup/format/v2_spec.md + tools/backup/create_v2_backup.go + tools/backup/restore_v2.go: define and implement the v2 backup format, including an encrypted secrets container compatible with KMS/HSM integration. 3) installer/migrations/runner.go: idempotent migrations with a dry-run flag and a deterministic lock file to prevent concurrent migration runs. 4) terraform/modules/version_pins.tf and charts/private/values.yaml: version pins for terraform providers and helm images used by the private installer. 5) ci/restore-e2e.yml: new CI matrix job which takes a v1.2 backup and attempts to restore it onto this branch and then back to v1.2 to validate downgrade guard behavior.

Testing performed: unit tests for migration runner (mocks for DB state), integration restore e2e that runs in GH Actions using a small ephemeral K3s cluster and mocked KMS provider, manual end-to-end restore/upgrade tests against a local vm cluster. All CI jobs passing in the PR run after a transient failure in the first integration run (flaky network to GH container registry) which was rerun.

Rollout plan: merge to main, cut v1.3.0-rc with the metadata file included. Default installer behavior remains conservative: the downgrade-blocker is enabled by default but can be temporarily relaxed with a documented admin flag --allow-downgrade-forced (requires explicit acceptance in audit logs). We will roll out the change to Dedicated customers on a staged schedule and provide a migration guide.

Rollback plan: installer will refuse to downgrade automatically; to recover a cluster that must be moved back to an older release we provide a controlled path: 1) take a v2 backup, 2) use a provided compatibility tool to map schema changes (installer/migrations/compatibility_mapper.go), 3) run restore with --force-downgrade (requires operator approval and generates a mandatory audit bundle). The force path is intentionally manual and logged.

Security notes: secrets in backups are encrypted using envelope encryption; restore tooling integrates with KMS via the existing KMS adapter. The downgrade blocker adds an operator-visible audit event.

Related: ENG-1182 (upgrade reliability), PM-431 (customer ask: guaranteed restore across minor versions).

Checklist: [x] migration runner unit tests, [x] backup/restore e2e, [x] helm/terraform pinning, [x] docs and upgrade guide, [x] audit events for forced-downgrade.

title: Sampled probe choreographer and on-call handoff scorecard
preview: Motivation: Reduce noisy probes and pager fatigue while improving first-responder context during handoffs by sampling probes intelligently and producing a compact handoff scorecard. This PR implements a sampled probe choreographer that schedules a small, representative set of lightweight probes per on-call window, tracks probe provenance, and emits a one-line handoff scorecard summarizing signal health and probe coverage. Changes include: 1) a new choreographer component (choreographer/sampled_scheduler.py) that picks probes deterministically using a weighted reservoir sampler tied to on-call rotation ids; 2) probe metadata propagation to the incident snapshot (snapshot/attachments.md -> snapshot metadata additions) so responders can see sample reason and TTL; 3) a handoff scorecard generator (handoff/scorecard.py) that produces a compact human-friendly summary and machine-readable tags for rollups; 4) a lightweight CLI to preview scheduled probes for the upcoming shift (bin/preview-probes) and a console widget to surface the scorecard in the on-call dashboard. Safety/perf: the choreographer enforces a configurable probe budget per rotation (default 8 probes per 6h window), backoff for noisy endpoints (exponential backoff on repeated failures), and samples across metadata dimensions (region, service, metric-family) to preserve coverage. Tests and CI: unit tests for sampler, deterministic-seeding tests, e2e simulated on-call run that verifies scorecard appears in handoff payloads. Rollout plan: feature gated behind incident_features.sampled_probes; gradual enablement by rotation -> team -> org with metrics monitored (probe hit rate, responder opens, pager volume). Checklist: - [x] design doc linked: https://linear.app/ENG/notes/sampled-probe-choreographer - [x] unit + integration tests - [x] add feature flag - [x] update docs and console widget - [x] added metrics/traces for rollout. Commits summary: "init sampled choreographer and basic scheduler"; "propagate probe metadata to snapshots"; "handoff scorecard formatting and tags"; "cli preview and console widget wiring"; "tests and docs". Changed files (high level): choreographer/sampled_scheduler.py, handoff/scorecard.py, snapshot/metadata_adapter.py, bin/preview-probes, web/ui/widgets/handoff_scorecard.vue, tests/test_sampler.py, docs/oncall/sampled-probes.md. Small illustrative snippet included in scheduler (single-line): "selection = reservoir.sample(candidates, budget, seed=rotation_id_hash)". Review convo summary: Ben Carter: "Can we ensure sampling is stable across rotation reassignments? If rotation id changes we'll lose continuity." Author reply: "Yes — scheduler seeds with rotation_id_hash derived from rotation uid and start timestamp; added deterministic-seeding tests (see test_sampler.py)." Maya Patel (requested changes): "Need clearer observability: add histogram for probe latency and a tag for sample_reason in traces." Author reply: "Added histograms and propagated sample_reason tag into trace spans; updated trace schema doc." Liam O'Connor: "Approve with minor nit on CLI help wording." Author reply: "Addressed nit and re-ran CI." CI: pipeline passed (unit+lint+integration); one flaky e2e rerun was required and passed on rerun. Merge outcome: merged by squash on 2026-02-25 (merge method: squash, merge commit message: "Sampled probe choreographer + handoff scorecard (ENG-3112)"). Release notes: "Added experimental sampled probe scheduling to reduce pager noise and a compact handoff scorecard that surfaces probe coverage and signal health. Feature gated and off by default." Additional notes: Monitoring dashboards created under SLO/Oncall (probe_budget_utilization, handoff_score_distribution). Follow-ups: instrument probe deduplication across overlapping rotations (ENG-3120), expand sampling policy to use anomaly-weighted scoring (SRE-47).

title: introduce-rag-microservice-tool-calling-docker-quickstart
preview: Add a compact, docker-first RAG microservice quickstart that demonstrates function/tool-calling, streaming responses, and a minimal KV cache for low-latency private deployments. Motivation: customers frequently ask for a small, deployable reference that shows how to wire Redwood SDK into a tiny service that handles document retrieval, model orchestration, and calls external tools (search + knowledge connector) with clear observability hooks. This PR delivers: 1) examples/rag-microservice/ - a ready-to-run template (Dockerfile + docker-compose) that boots a single-node instance with an embedded retriever, Redwood SDK integration, and a simple tool interface for invoking a mock search API. 2) README quickstart that walks through local Docker run, env var configuration for hosted vs. private endpoints, and sample curl/SDK calls for streaming. 3) src/agent.ts - an opinionated agent orchestration example that shows stepwise tool-calling (retrieve -> call tool -> synthesize -> stream) and includes backpressure-friendly streaming hooks. 4) docs/quickstarts/rag-microservice.md - a short doc page for the docs site (to be pulled into the quickstarts index) focused on deployment modes and production considerations (batching, KV cache warmup, model pinning). 5) Integration test and CI job to validate startup and a basic end-to-end conversation sample. Checklist: - [x] Minimal runnable example (Docker) - [x] Clear env var examples for redwood API key / private endpoint - [x] Streaming example with SDK snippets for JS and Python - [x] Observability pointers (which telemetry hooks to wire) - [x] Integration test in CI that runs container and POSTs a sample query Notes on differences vs existing RAG quickstarts: this template is intentionally compact and implements tool-calling as explicit function stubs (mock_search, metadata_indexer) so customers can replace with their production connectors easily. It emphasizes local Docker-first flow and opts for a lightweight on-disk document index rather than a full vector DB to keep the quickstart low-friction. Compatibility: works with Redwood SDK >= 0.9.0; recommends model variants that support streaming token-level responses. Backwards-incompatible changes: none. Related Linear tasks: ENG-4991 implement micro-quickstart; PM-221 doc alignment for quickstarts. Merge rationale: small, self-contained, high-value example for engineering onboarding and support triage.

title: capacity-leasebook-and-throttle-harmonizer-for-stable-dedicated-throughput
preview: Context: Dedicated customers observed P95 latency spikes during short high-frequency bursts that consumed warm-pool headroom and caused aggressive preemption of committed capacity; this PR introduces a Capacity Leasebook and a Throttle Harmonizer to smooth those microbursts and preserve long-running throughput guarantees. Motivation: decouple short-lived burst reservations from sustained commitments so that priority tenants see consistent throughput while still allowing opportunistic short bursts. High-level changes: new leasebook controller that issues timeboxed 'leases' for burst capacity; soft-lease arbitration algorithm (probabilistic decay + runway smoothing) to avoid synchronized drain; harmonizer in scheduler that computes gentle per-tenant throttles when short-lease utilization exceeds configured runway thresholds; telemetry for per-lease lifespan histograms, preemption counts, effective throughput by commit window, and headroom pressure signals. Rollout plan: feature-flagged (config.redwood.enableLeasebook=false by default), opt-in migration tool for existing reservations, canary on 10% of Dedicated clusters, full rollout after 2 weeks if no regressions. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (internal) and linear tickets referenced; - [x] unit tests for lease allocator and harmonizer; - [x] deterministic integration test for preemption/backoff scenarios; - [x] load tests showing P50/P95 SLO improvement for committed tenants; - [x] console metrics and dashboards updated; - [ ] staged rollout recipe applied to production fleets. Commits summary: 1) Add leasebook controller, CRD, and api types (6 files) 2) Implement soft-lease allocator and decay policy with tunables (3 files) 3) Integrate harmonizer with scheduler decision path + tests (4 files) 4) Add telemetry hooks, dashboards, and migration script (3 files) 5) Docs, changelog, and rollout notes (2 files). Changed areas (high level): pkg/controller/leasebook/*, pkg/scheduler/harmonizer.go, pkg/scheduler/decision.go (throttle hooks), pkg/telemetry/lease_metrics.go, deploy/helm/values.dedicated.yaml (feature flag), tools/migrate-reservations.sh, docs/dedicated/leasebook.md. Test plan and CI: initial CI run flagged a flaky util test (util/alloc_test) at first; reran unit and integration suites; all checks green on second run; e2e burst load validated on a 4-node Dedicated canary (results attached in PR CI artifacts). Review conversation summary: "Rohit Singh: please bound per-lease memory to avoid unbounded metadata growth;" -> "Author: added a bounded ring for short-lived leases and unit test exercising high churn;" "Lena Ortiz: add tenant and reservation tags to metrics for easier pivoting in console;" -> "Author: added tenant_id and reservation_type labels and updated dashboards;" "Ben Carter: can you provide a canary rollout recipe and rollback plan?" -> "Author: included canary recipe and automatic-disable hook, plus migration script that is idempotent;" Approvals: Rohit and Lena approved after requested changes implemented; Ben approved after canary notes. CI status: pass (lint/unit/integration/e2e). Merge: squash-and-merge by Maya Chen after approvals and green CI; merge commit: abcdef1. Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the default flag off to avoid surprise behavior for existing Dedicated customers; telemetry keys are versioned so downstream alerts won't break on rollout.

title: Orchestrated A/B-Test Anchoring with Fast-Fallback and promotion hooks
preview:
Motivation: Teams running A/B tests and canary promotions need deterministic cohort anchoring plus an automated fast-fallback when latency, error-rate, or quality regressions are detected. This PR introduces a controller and API surface that: 1) pins user/query cohorts to a stable treatment (anchor) during long-running A/B experiments, 2) provides promotion hooks to move anchored cohorts from shadow->canary->primary on a schedule or metric gate, and 3) implements a fast-fallback path that immediately drains traffic from a failing variant and re-anchors affected cohorts to the last known-good variant.

Changes include: controller implementation (orchestration/scheduler), a new lightweight cohort store (kv-backed, optional in Dedicated/Private), API surface for experiments (create/update/pause/promote), telemetry and probe instrumentation (per-cohort latency/error/quality counters), and end-to-end integration tests and docs. The description below summarizes the components and rationale.

Design highlights: - Cohort anchoring: a deterministic hashing and sticky assignment ensures the same caller/session lands in the same treatment for the experiment duration. Anchors are stored with TTL and versioned keys so rollbacks can replay previous assignments. - Promotion hooks: experiments can declare promotion policies (time-based, metric threshold, linear ramp). Promotions go through a two-phase commit: dry-run observation then switch, with a snapshot of cohort mapping. - Fast-fallback: when a policy gate trips (latency > SLO, error spike, or model-quality delta beyond threshold), the controller triggers immediate drain of failing variant and reassigns cohorts to the last healthy variant; a short-lived quarantine lane accepts replayed requests for diagnostics. - Safety and audit: all assignment changes and fallback events are journaled; operators can view the journal and roll forward/back via the console or API.

Backward compatibility: experiments default to the older routing behavior if no experiment config is present. The cohort store is optional — when disabled the controller falls back to probabilistic assignment but still enforces promotion/fallback flows.

Testing and rollout: unit and integration tests added under integrations/ab_anchor. Performance microbenchmarks show assignment latency <200us p95 and fallback completion time under 2s in a local Dedicated environment.

Why this is different from prior rollout work: prior PRs focused on traffic-splitting and staged canaries; this adds persistent cohort assignment and a fast, auditable emergency fallback designed for long-lived A/B experiments where reassigning users mid-experiment is unacceptable.

Related: Linear ENG-2345 (AB experiment lifecycle), ENG-2419 (fast-fallback SLO integration).

title: introduce-tranche-econ-tiler-prefill-optimizer-and-cli
preview:
Motivation: Tenant workloads show multimodal sequence-length and arrival distributions; static batch/caching defaults lead to suboptimal unit economics. This PR introduces the Tranche Econ Tiler (TET) — a runtime component plus a small CLI to infer tenant-prefill "tranches" (batch shape + KV-cache allocation + quant tier) from historical traffic and cost signals, and emit safe defaults for the autoscaler and cost-suggester.

What this changes: Adds a new runtime component (tranche-tiler) that runs as part of the prefill pipeline. TET: (1) ingests sample windows, (2) computes candidate tranche templates using a tiling strategy that balances latency SLOs vs tokens/unit cost, (3) scores candidates with the unit-econ evaluator, and (4) exposes an HTTP endpoint and a CLI to export curated defaults as YAML presets. The CLI supports dry-run mode and a deterministic seed to reproduce tranche generation.

Key features: safe fallback to existing autopilot defaults; explicit quant-tier suggestions (fp16/int8/awq-based hints) tied to a conservative quality budget; cache-budget mapper to allocate KV cache capacity per tranche; lightweight telemetry counters (tranche.created, tranche.applied, tranche.fallback) and unit-econ drift metrics.

Why different from previous work: existing PRs tuned batch/caching heuristics at global or tenant-profile level. TET introduces hierarchical "tranches" so one tenant can have multiple coexisting templates (short-form, interactive; long-form, batch) and the runtime will route requests to the most cost-effective tranche without breaking existing SLO routing rules. It emphasizes reproducible presets-as-code that can be reviewed in Git.

Compatibility and safety: Generated presets are opt-in by default. When applied in Managed/Dedicated environments the runtime performs an A/B rollout (canary 5%, 25%, 100%) with automatic rollback triggers tied to latency or quality regressions. The PR wires into existing rollback hooks.

Testing and rollout plan: unit tests for tiler heuristics + end-to-end integration tests with prefiller harness included. CI runs a small synthetic traffic replay to validate no regressions at scale. We plan a staged rollout: internal dogfood -> beta customers on Dedicated -> default opt-in after 4 weeks of telemetry.

Notes: this PR contains a new small CLI binary (redwood-tiler) and a migration that adds a new config section to defaults/cost_profiles.yaml. Please focus reviews on runtime contention points and the safe-fallback orchestration logic.

title: add-invocation-telemetry-deduper-and-token-collapse-visualizer
preview: Introduce a lightweight Telemetry Deduper service and a Token Collapse Visualizer dashboard to reduce noisy duplicate spans and surface redundant token-level cost patterns. Motivation: during several incidents and routine SLO tuning we observed high cardinality and alert fatigue caused by duplicated spans emitted from the serving runtime (frontend retries, duplicate instrumentation points, and batched replays). This PR implements a deterministic fingerprinting pipeline that collapses semantically-identical spans early in the observability ingestion path and a dashboard that helps engineers understand when token costs are dominated by collapsed/duplicate traffic. The change includes a TTL-backed fingerprint cache, session-aware collapse heuristics, an orthogonal latency decomposition helper ("orthogonalizer") that attributes latency to queue/io/model phases for collapsed invocations, alert tuning rules to avoid duplicate alert storms, end-to-end tests with synthetic replay, and a Grafana dashboard with an interactive token-collapse Sankey and cost-savings calculator. Backwards compatibility: original spans are still retained in sampled snapshots; deduped counts are emitted as both raw_count and deduped_count so downstream consumers can opt-in. This description includes: design rationale, implementation summary, QA notes, rollout plan, and linked Linear/Jira items.

title: Add telemetry events + dashboard hooks for marketplace onboarding funnel
preview:
Context: As part of the Cloud Marketplace Listing Refresh, we need reliable, provider-consistent instrumentation to measure the subscription → entitlement → org provisioning → first API key/usage funnel and identify drop-offs. Today we have partial logs and a few ad-hoc metrics, but no stable analytics taxonomy and no standard error/latency dimensions to support Partnerships + Growth reporting. This PR introduces a small set of canonical marketplace onboarding telemetry events emitted by the console + control-plane API, plus a metrics bridge so we can build dashboards/alerts without scraping logs.

Summary of changes: (1) Adds canonical analytics events for marketplace onboarding across AWS/GCP flows. (2) Adds latency + error code dimensions for entitlement verification and org linking steps. (3) Adds a lightweight server-side aggregator that emits Prometheus counters/histograms to support operational dashboards ("entitlement check failure rate", "entitlement check p95") while keeping product analytics in RudderStack/warehouse. (4) Adds dashboard hook definitions (Grafana JSON + Looker explore notes) and updates the event catalog docs.

Event taxonomy (new): All events are emitted as "track" events to telemetry with a stable schema. Event names are provider-agnostic; provider is a property.
- marketplace.onboarding_entrypoint_viewed (when user enters marketplace-specific console flow)
- marketplace.entitlement_check_started
- marketplace.entitlement_check_succeeded
- marketplace.entitlement_check_failed
- marketplace.org_link_started
- marketplace.org_link_succeeded
- marketplace.org_link_failed
- marketplace.api_key_create_started
- marketplace.api_key_create_succeeded
- marketplace.api_key_create_failed
- marketplace.first_request_succeeded (first successful request made with a marketplace-provisioned key)
- marketplace.provisioning_waiting_shown (UI state when entitlement exists but org provisioning not yet completed)

Common properties (all events): provider (aws|gcp), provider_account_id_hash (sha256, salted), marketplace_product_code (string, where available), marketplace_sku_id (Redwood marketplace SKU id), marketplace_offer_type (public|private|unknown), region (control plane region), environment (prod|staging), redwood_org_id (uuid), redwood_user_id (uuid), session_id, request_id (if server-side), error_code (if failed), error_stage (entitlement|org_link|api_key|first_request), latency_ms (when applicable). We intentionally do NOT send raw cloud account IDs, emails, API keys, or full error messages. Error details are bucketed by code.

Operational metrics (Prometheus):
- marketplace_entitlement_check_total{provider,result,error_code}
- marketplace_entitlement_check_latency_ms_bucket{provider,result}
- marketplace_org_link_total{provider,result,error_code}
- marketplace_org_link_latency_ms_bucket{provider,result}
- marketplace_api_key_create_total{provider,result,error_code}
- marketplace_first_request_total{provider}

Dashboards/hooks: Adds Grafana dashboard skeleton "Marketplace Onboarding Funnel (Ops)" with panels for p50/p95 entitlement check latency, error-rate by code, and time-in-waiting-state distribution. Looker/warehouse note added for funnel chart using events marketplace.onboarding_entrypoint_viewed → marketplace.entitlement_check_succeeded → marketplace.org_link_succeeded → marketplace.api_key_create_succeeded → marketplace.first_request_succeeded.

Implementation notes:
- Console now emits client-side events on key steps, but all success/failure completion events are confirmed from server responses so the funnel is not inflated by navigation abandons.
- Control-plane emits server-side events for entitlement verification endpoints (ENG-22110) and org linking endpoints; these are used for authoritative latency/error metrics.
- Adds a shared error mapping table (MarketplaceOnboardingErrorCode) to keep error codes consistent across UI + API.
- Rate limiting: server-side telemetry emission uses a bounded async queue; drops are counted in marketplace_telemetry_dropped_total.

Testing:
- Unit tests for event payload construction and redaction (no raw provider account id).
- Integration tests for entitlement check endpoint instrumentation (ensures error_code mapping on common failures).
- Manual: validated staging flow for AWS and GCP subscription linking, including "entitlement propagation delay" UI state.

Rollout:
- Behind feature flag telemetry.marketplace_onboarding_v1 (default ON in staging, OFF in prod).
- After merge: enable in prod at 25% for 24h, then 100% if no telemetry drop spikes and no PII violations in warehouse sampling.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Add client-side retry reason fields for entitlement propagation delay (ENG-22116 / sync job hardening) once final error codes are agreed.
- Wire dashboards to SRE alert thresholds after baseline is collected for ~7 days.

Checklist: [x] Reviewed with Growth (Ben) for funnel semantics [x] Reviewed with Console owner (Chloe) for UX coverage [x] Reviewed with Billing/Metering (Cole) for SKU id properties [x] Security review for PII/redaction (Marcus to follow up in separate security docs PR) [x] Added docs to internal event catalog

title: Add AWS/GCP Marketplace onboarding pages + entitlement troubleshooting
preview:
Summary: This PR adds first-class AWS and GCP Marketplace onboarding docs to reduce time-to-first-token for marketplace buyers and to unblock partner submission requirements. It introduces a Marketplace onboarding landing page, provider-specific step-by-step guides (Subscribe → Entitlement → Provision org → Create API key → First request), and an entitlement troubleshooting page with concrete error states and recommended next actions. It also links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack and clarifies which security artifacts are public vs available on request.

Motivation / context: Marketplace buyers are procurement-led and frequently get stuck during entitlement propagation and org linking. Partner feedback requested clearer customer-facing steps and explicit guidance for common failure modes (e.g., existing orgs, multi-subscription, delayed entitlement sync). This doc set is intended to pair with the console marketplace entrypoint (ENG-22112) and entitlement verification endpoint work (ENG-22110) by documenting the same error codes/UX copy.

What’s included: (1) New docs IA for Marketplace onboarding under /docs/marketplace/ with a landing page that routes customers by provider and deployment type (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). (2) AWS Marketplace guide covering subscription, private offers vs public offers, entitlement timing expectations, how to find product code / account identifiers needed for support, and first-call examples. (3) GCP Marketplace guide covering subscription, billing account prerequisites, procurement approvals, and first-call examples. (4) Troubleshooting: a dedicated Entitlements & provisioning troubleshooting page with a table mapping common errors (ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND, ENTITLEMENT_PENDING, ORG_ALREADY_LINKED, SUBSCRIPTION_MISMATCH, REGION_NOT_AVAILABLE) to remediation steps, including when to wait vs when to contact support and what data to provide. (5) Security: links to the Marketplace Security Documentation Pack index and a short ‘Security & compliance for marketplace buyers’ section with safe wording (no over-claims), plus notes on audit logs and retention controls.

Out of scope: Pricing details beyond high-level SKU descriptions (handled in the pricing/packaging pages update PR). No changes to console UX or backend entitlement logic.

Notes for partner submission: Final URLs introduced in this PR are stable and intended to be used in AWS/GCP marketplace listing fields. If redirects/vanity URLs are needed for older links, we should track separately (see INT-771).

QA: Ran local docs build, link checker, and spell/markdown lint. Verified nav placement and breadcrumbs. Confirmed that security links do not expose gated evidence directly and instead route to the security pack overview with ‘contact us’/‘on request’ language for reports.

Screenshots: Not included in this PR; screenshots will land once Design finalizes the capture set (DES-913).

title: Add minimal chat app template to accelerate Hosted API activation
preview:
This PR adds a small, copy-pasteable "minimal chat app" example intended to reduce time-to-first-token for new Hosted API users. The goal is to provide a single folder that can be run in <5 minutes with an API key, supports streaming by default, and matches the onboarding email + console nudge CTAs ("Make your first request" / "Enable streaming"). It also includes lightweight telemetry guidance so the Growth funnel can attribute activation accelerators without collecting sensitive payloads.

What’s included: (1) New example at examples/minimal-chat-app/ implemented as a Node + Express server with a tiny static UI. Server proxies to Redwood Hosted API using the Redwood TS SDK, streams tokens to the browser via SSE, and shows basic error states (missing/invalid API key, rate limited). (2) README updates: top-level README now highlights the minimal chat app as the recommended first project for Hosted API onboarding; the example README includes a “First request” section, troubleshooting, and a short note on best practices (do not expose API keys in the browser, rotate keys, prefer server-side calls). (3) Telemetry guidance: adds a section describing optional instrumentation events aligned with the activation funnel taxonomy (e.g., example_app_started, example_first_request_sent, example_streaming_enabled, example_error_seen) and explicitly states that prompts/completions must not be logged. Telemetry is opt-in via an env var and defaults to off in the template.

Why: During the lifecycle growth experiments sprint, we observed that many new signups stall between API key creation and first successful request. A minimal runnable template is a high-leverage way to reduce friction, and it creates a stable target for onboarding emails and console nudges to link to.

Notes/guardrails: The template intentionally avoids any client-side Redwood calls to prevent key leakage. The telemetry examples only include non-content metadata (timestamps, booleans, coarse error categories) and are documented as optional. No changes to production code; examples repo only.

Rollout: Once merged, Marketing/DevEx will update onboarding email CTAs to deep-link into the minimal chat app README and pin it in the console onboarding checklist. Follow-up task (separate) will add a Docker option if we see install issues on Windows.

title: Add “Hello world” first request snippet aligned with activation CTAs
preview:
## Context
This sprint’s primary activation KPI is “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” within 7 days. We’ve seen drop-off at the earliest step due to (a) too many options in the quickstart, (b) mismatch between lifecycle email CTAs and what the docs show, and (c) missing/unclear env var and model defaults.

This PR adds a minimal, copy/paste “Hello world” snippet that matches the onboarding email v2 + console onboarding checklist CTA wording: “Make your first request.” The snippet is intentionally small: create API key → export env var → single streaming request.

## What changed
- New `hello-world` page that is optimized for TTFT (goal: <2 minutes from API key creation to first streamed token).
- Adds 3 variants with identical structure:
  - curl (streaming)
  - Node/TypeScript (SDK)
  - Python (SDK)
- Standardizes naming used across growth surfaces:
  - Env var: `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
  - Base URL: `https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1`
  - Model example: `redwood/verified-llm-small` (placeholder here is a stable, low-latency model option used in onboarding)
- Adds a small “Common errors” callout focused on the top early failures we’ve seen (missing key / 401, model typo / 404, network / proxy).
- Adds UTM-safe anchors for lifecycle email deep links and console CTA targets (no JS; just stable fragment ids).

## Example (curl)
```bash
export REDWOOD_API_KEY="<your_api_key>"

curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/verified-llm-small",
    "stream": true,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello in one sentence."}]
  }'
```

## Measurement / instrumentation notes
- This repo doesn’t emit product events, but the snippet is designed to correspond cleanly to funnel events being instrumented in the API/console (ENG-10492): `api_key_created` → `first_request_succeeded`.
- The page includes consistent CTA copy to reduce confusion in qualitative feedback and support tickets.

## QA
- Verified commands work end-to-end against staging.
- Ensured snippets are mobile-friendly (short lines, minimal wrapping).
- Confirmed no mention of experimental features or non-default flags.

## Rollout
- Safe to merge immediately; Growth will link to this page in lifecycle email v2 + console nudge CTAs via existing docs PRs.

## Screenshots
- N/A (markdown-only change)

title: Reduce CPU syscalls and thread handoff on request path
preview: Motivation: CPU-side bottlenecks on the request hot path were dominated by frequent syscalls and expensive thread handoffs when requests crossed the network/IO boundary. This PR reduces syscall frequency and simplifies handoff between acceptor/dispatcher threads and worker pools to lower tail latency and overall CPU usage. Summary of approach: 1) Replace a large fraction of blocking read()/write()/select() syscalls on the hot path with an edge-triggered epoll-based poll loop + per-core submission queues to amortize kernel transitions; 2) Introduce a light-weight per-core MPMC ring buffer (lock-free where supported) used to hand off ready requests to CPU-local worker shards instead of parking/unparking threads; 3) Move request serialization (marshalling) off the acceptor path onto a short-lived serialization worker that batches small requests together (continuous batching window = 1.5ms configurable) to reduce per-request syscall and kernel scheduling pressure; 4) Make KV cache lookups prefetch-friendly and avoid cacheline thrash by introducing per-shard micro-ttl caches and partitioned LRU lists; 5) Reduce futex/wake cycles by aggregating wake events using a shared event-coalescer and using eventfd on Linux where available. Results: On our internal bench (conv1k latency-heavy mix) we see a 28-35% reduction in 99th percentile CPU time spent in syscall/wakeup handling and a 12-18% improvement in overall token throughput on c5d-large simulation (worker-limited). Tail latency (p99.9) decreased by ~9% for small requests (<128 tokens). Backwards compatibility and safety: All changes are behind a feature flag (runtime.enable_syscall_reduction) and fall back to the original path when disabled. New primitives are exercised via unit + integration tests, and we added a runtime smoke test that toggles the feature flag under load. Details of changes: commits, files touched, and notes below.

title: Add analytics events for benchmark page interactions + conversions
preview:
Context
We need consistent, queryable analytics for the public benchmark landing page + interactive table so Marketing Ops can report on benchmark launch KPIs (engagement → signup/demo) and Sales can see top-of-funnel intent signals. This PR adds client-side instrumentation for the key interactions called out in PM-826.

What this PR does
- Adds a small benchmark-specific event schema (packages/analytics/src/events/benchmark.ts) and emits events from the benchmark page UI.
- Emits events for:
  - Filter changes (model, region, context length bucket)
  - Metric toggle changes (latency/$ vs p95 latency vs $/1M tokens)
  - Export clicks (CSV, JSON)
  - CTA clicks (Start free trial, Request demo, Dedicated sizing)
- Normalizes payload fields so downstream dashboards don’t require bespoke parsing.
- Includes basic payload validation + unit tests to prevent accidental breaking changes.

Event names + payloads
All events are emitted via the existing `track()` client wrapper and include the common context (session_id, page_path, referrer, utm_* where present).

1) benchmark_filter_change
- model: string | null
- region: string | null
- context_length_bucket: one of ["<=2k", "2k-8k", ">8k"] | null
- metric: one of ["latency_per_dollar", "p95_latency_ms", "cost_per_1m_tokens"]

2) benchmark_metric_toggle
- from_metric: string
- to_metric: string

3) benchmark_export_click
- export_format: one of ["csv", "json"]
- rows_exported: number (best-effort; based on current table state)

4) benchmark_cta_click
- cta: one of ["start_trial", "request_demo", "dedicated_sizing"]
- destination: string (relative URL)
- placement: one of ["hero", "sticky", "below_table"]

Implementation notes
- We debounce filter-change emissions by 250ms so quick successive toggles don’t generate a burst of events.
- We intentionally do NOT log any table cell values or benchmark numbers (only the UI selections). This keeps event payloads stable and avoids accidentally leaking unpublished/experimental results.
- We gate tracking behind `PUBLIC_ANALYTICS_ENABLED` and no-op in local dev unless explicitly enabled.
- Export events fire on user click (not on file download completion). CSV/JSON generation is client-side; there’s no server callback to measure completion reliably.

Testing
- Unit tests validate event payload shape and enum values.
- Manual test steps:
  1) Load /benchmarks?utm_source=test
  2) Change model + region filters → see benchmark_filter_change with expected payloads
  3) Toggle metric → benchmark_metric_toggle
  4) Export CSV/JSON → benchmark_export_click
  5) Click each CTA → benchmark_cta_click

Screenshots / snippets
Example emission (BenchmarkFilters.tsx):
- track('benchmark_filter_change', { model, region, context_length_bucket, metric })

Follow-ups
- Hook these events into HubSpot campaign tagging (INT-9322) and the post-launch KPI dashboard spec (google drive: post-launch-metrics-dashboard-spec).
- If we add an in-console benchmark view later, we should reuse the same event schema + names for apples-to-apples reporting.

Risk
Low. This is additive instrumentation behind an env gate with payload validation + tests.

Checklist
- [x] Event names reviewed with Marketing Ops (Tyler) + Growth (Ben)
- [x] Payload does not include benchmark values
- [x] Tests added
- [x] Docs added (internal event dictionary)

title: Add feature flag + cohorting rules for Console onboarding checklist (with kill switch)
preview: Context: We’re rolling out the new onboarding / “first production launch” checklist in Console behind a feature flag, with progressive exposure by cohort. This PR introduces the first-cut flag definition, deterministic cohort assignment, and an emergency kill-switch path that Support/SRE can use if we see regressions during dogfood or cohort rollout. Summary of changes: (1) Adds flag definition `console.onboarding_checklist` with structured config: `enabled`, `mode`, `allowlist_tenants`, `denylist_tenants`, `rollout_percentage`, `cohort_seed`, and `kill_switch` (server-side hard disable). (2) Implements deterministic cohort assignment using stable hashing on `tenant_id` + `cohort_seed` to avoid cohort “flapping” between requests and across regions. (3) Adds a small policy layer to compute exposure in the control plane so Console can request “shouldShowChecklist” with a single API call (rather than duplicating logic client-side). (4) Adds operational guardrails: kill-switch can be toggled via config service and also via `ONBOARDING_CHECKLIST_KILL_SWITCH=1` env var override for emergency response. (5) Adds telemetry events for exposure decisions (aggregated, low-cardinality) to support rollout monitoring: `onboarding_checklist.exposure_evaluated` with fields `{tenant_id_hash, decision, reason, rollout_percentage, mode}`. (Tenant ID is hashed server-side; no org name/email/etc.) Rollout modes: `off` (default), `dogfood` (employee + internal tenants only), `cohort` (percentage-based), `allowlist` (explicit tenants only). Precedence (highest to lowest): kill_switch -> denylist -> allowlist -> mode/percentage. Notes / non-goals: This PR does not include checklist UI (ENG-5814) or progress tracking service changes (ENG-5813). It only provides a consistent mechanism for exposure + safe rollback. Related: PM-768 (Rollout plan and feature flag strategy). Testing: Added unit tests for cohort determinism, precedence ordering, and env-var kill switch. Added integration test to validate consistent exposure decision across regions when config is replicated. Follow-ups: (a) Hook Console UI gating to the new `GET /console/feature-flags` endpoint (tracked in ENG-5814). (b) Add an admin “force enable for tenant” override path for Support (separate PR / ENG-5852).

title: router: add FPE decision tracing + enforce pinned-build fallback guardrails
tenant-buffer-pool-rebalancer-and-utilization-signal-mediator
preview: Motivation: tenants with bursty traffic were observed to induce oscillatory reassignments of reserved buffer credits during contention windows which degraded P99 latency for mid-tier customers. This PR introduces a conservative, proportional rebalancer for tenant buffer pools combined with a utilization-signal mediation layer that smooths short-term noise and applies a fairness gate to protect latency-sensitive SLOs. The change is opt-in behind a feature flag and includes backward-compatible fallbacks to the existing allocation ledger. Implementation summary: 1) controller: tenant_buffer_rebalancer - samples per-tenant occupancy and in-flight rates, computes target deltas using a damped proportional allocator, and emits reassignments via the existing reservation API; 2) util-signal-mediator: lightweight EWMA smoothing with configurable half-life and an outlier clamp that prevents a single spike from triggering rebalances; 3) fairness gate: when a tenant's latency SLO metric cross-check fails, rebalancer reduces reclamation aggressiveness for that tenant; 4) telemetry: new prometheus metrics (rb_rebalance_attempts_total, rb_rebalance_succeeded_total, rb_util_ewma, rb_fairness_skips_total) and structured tracing spans to link rebalances to tenant traces; 5) migration: small idempotent background job to reconcile existing reservation ledgers to the new buffer accounting (no data loss). The description below enumerates commits, changed paths, CI results, and the review thread. This PR is targeted to Dedicated capacity and fleet management improvements and aims to decrease P99 tail latency regressions under multi-tenant contention while keeping burst capacity utilization high.
